%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Pole (or Zero) at the Origin

> **Section Objective:** Learn how a pole or zero at the origin appears on Bode magnitude and phase plots.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- log-frequency axis
- pole at the origin magnitude
- zero at the origin magnitude
- constant phase contribution
- dB per decade and dB per octave

## 1. The log-frequency axis

Bode plots use a **logarithmic horizontal axis**, meaning equal distances on the axis represent equal *ratios* of frequency, not equal differences. Here, \(\omega\) is angular frequency (rad/s) and \(u\) is the corresponding log-scale coordinate.

**Minimal example:**
- \(\omega = 1 \Rightarrow u = 0\)
- \(\omega = 10 \Rightarrow u = 1\)
- \(\omega = 0.1 \Rightarrow u = -1\)

**When to use this:** Any time a slope is described in dB per decade or dB per octave, you are working on this log axis.

### EXAM TRIGGER
A semilog Bode plot with frequency ratios — recognize that one unit on the horizontal axis means \(\omega\) is multiplied by 10.

### COMMON MISTAKE
Treating one unit on the horizontal axis as \(\omega\) increasing by 1 (arithmetic) instead of multiplying by 10 (logarithmic).

$$u = \log_{10}\omega$$

## 2. Pole at the origin: magnitude

This formula converts a **frequency ratio** into a distance on the log axis. Here \(\omega_1\) and \(\omega_2\) are two frequencies, and \(u_1\), \(u_2\) are their positions on the log axis.

Two textbook facts to memorize:
- **One decade:** \(\omega_2/\omega_1 = 10\), so \(u_2 - u_1 = 1\)
- **One octave:** \(\omega_2/\omega_1 = 2\), so \(u_2 - u_1 = \log_{10}2 \approx 0.3010\)

### COMMON MISTAKE
Calling any fixed frequency *difference* a decade. A decade is a frequency *ratio* of 10, not an arithmetic gap.

$$u_2 - u_1 = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)$$


## 3. Zero at the origin: magnitude

A pole at the origin contributes a **negative log-magnitude term** to the Bode plot. Here \(j\) is the imaginary-axis factor, \(\omega\) is angular frequency, and the expression gives the dB magnitude contribution of the origin pole.

Because \(|j\omega| = \omega\) for positive frequency, the Bode magnitude is a **straight line** on the log-frequency axis.

**Exam result:**
- Slope \(= -20\) dB/decade \(\approx -6\) dB/octave
- The line crosses **0 dB at \(\omega = 1\)**

**Minimal example:** Increasing \(\omega\) from 1 to 10 (one decade) decreases the contribution from 0 dB to \(-20\) dB.

### COMMON MISTAKE
Drawing a curved line, or using \(+20\) dB/decade for a pole. The pole gives a *negative* slope.

$$-20\log_{10}|j\omega| = -20\log_{10}\omega$$

## 4. Phase: pole versus zero at the origin

A zero at the origin has the **same straight-line shape** as the pole case but with the **opposite sign**. Here \(\omega\) is angular frequency and the expression is the dB magnitude contribution of the origin zero.

**Exam result:**
- Slope \(= +20\) dB/decade \(\approx +6\) dB/octave
- The line crosses **0 dB at \(\omega = 1\)**

**Minimal example:** Increasing \(\omega\) from 1 to 10 (one decade) raises the contribution from 0 dB to \(+20\) dB.

The zero magnitude line is the **mirror image** of the pole magnitude line about the 0 dB axis.

### COMMON MISTAKE
Remembering the 20 but forgetting that the sign depends on whether it is a pole (negative) or a zero (positive).

$$20\log_{10}\omega$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 4. Phase: pole versus zero at the origin

A pole at the origin contributes a **constant phase of \(-90^\circ\)** for all \(\omega\). Here \(\angle H(j\omega)\) is the phase contribution and \(\angle(j\omega)\) is the angle of the \(j\omega\) factor, which is always \(+90^\circ\) for positive frequency.

The paired zero result: a zero at the origin contributes **\(+90^\circ\)** for all \(\omega\).

| Factor | Phase contribution |
|--------|-------------------|
| Pole at origin | \(-90^\circ\) (constant) |
| Zero at origin | \(+90^\circ\) (constant) |

### EXAM TRIGGER
If the factor is at the origin, the phase plot is a **horizontal line** — not a transition curve.

### COMMON MISTAKE
Drawing a gradual phase transition from \(0^\circ\) to \(-90^\circ\) as if this were a nonzero break-frequency factor. The origin factor has no corner frequency, so the phase is flat across all frequencies.

$$\angle H(j\omega) = -\angle(j\omega) = -90^\circ$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **Log-frequency axis:** \(u = \log_{10}\omega\). Equal distances = equal frequency ratios. One decade: \(\omega_2/\omega_1 = 10\), \(u_2 - u_1 = 1\). One octave: \(\omega_2/\omega_1 = 2\), \(u_2 - u_1 \approx 0.3010\).
- **Frequency ratio to axis distance:** \(u_2 - u_1 = \log_{10}(\omega_2/\omega_1)\).
- **Pole at origin — magnitude:** \(-20\log_{10}|j\omega| = -20\log_{10}\omega\). Slope \(= -20\) dB/decade \(\approx -6\) dB/octave. Crosses 0 dB at \(\omega = 1\).
- **Zero at origin — magnitude:** \(20\log_{10}\omega\). Slope \(= +20\) dB/decade \(\approx +6\) dB/octave. Crosses 0 dB at \(\omega = 1\). Mirror image of the pole line.
- **Pole at origin — phase:** \(\angle H(j\omega) = -90^\circ\) (constant, horizontal line for all \(\omega\)).
- **Zero at origin — phase:** \(+90^\circ\) (constant, horizontal line for all \(\omega\)). No gradual transition — origin factors have no corner frequency.

*Next, you will use these basic origin factors as building blocks inside larger Bode plots.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
